A group is trying to evaluate candidate explanations before the equations are written down. They know this is an open question, not a settled ability, because in physics a verbal idea often lives or dies only once it is mathematically articulated. So they can discuss novelty in advance, but they cannot assume that such judgments are reliable in the same way they would be after formalization.

They also have a method on the table: make discovery tractable by narrowing the search space. That may help, but they also accept that this cannot be the whole story. Some real discoveries may need the opposite move first: a temporary widening into bizarre ontologies, with narrowing only later. So any procedure that treats narrowing alone as sufficient risks excluding the very cases it hopes to find.

At the same time, the group classifies ideas using a background pool, a universal pool, and a novel pool. But they recognize a contamination problem. Anything assigned to the novel pool may secretly be only a reformulation of material already present in the background pool. Because unnoticed reformulation is possible, membership in the novel pool cannot by itself prove genuine novelty.

Now add the remaining true sentences: “Slubbering lethargically sheared distributed unduke” and “Reconciliating ensignhood halophilous woodsman soreheaded.” Since they are given as true premises, they belong to the total set of truths the group must accommodate. But nothing in the other premises provides a mathematical articulation, ontology, translation rule, or pool-assignment criterion for them. They may be meaningful, or they may be disguised reformulations, or they may be candidates from some widened bizarre ontology. Before formal articulation, the group cannot decisively tell which.

That creates a pressure test for their method. If they narrow too early, they may discard those sentences as unusable noise and thereby risk missing a discovery that required temporary widening. If they widen, they can keep the sentences in play, but then the ambiguity of the pools prevents them from confidently calling them novel; they may merely restate something already in the background in an unnoticed way. And because explanatory novelty before mathematical articulation is an open question, they also cannot confidently rank the sentences as explanatorily significant or insignificant on verbal inspection alone.

So the world implied by these premises is one in which pre-mathematical evaluation is possible only under acknowledged uncertainty. Search must sometimes widen before it narrows. Novelty labels are unstable because reformulation can masquerade as invention. And at least some true statements can enter the scene without any available basis for deciding, in advance of mathematics, whether they are deep breakthroughs, contaminated reformulations, or just truths awaiting the articulation that would sort them.
